note: Under the Copyright Act, the employer or commissioning party is considered the author (and owner) of a work made for hire, unless the parties expressly agree otherwise in a signed writing.

citation: Community for Creative Non-Violence v. Reid, 490 U.S. 730 (1989)
note: Established the multi-factor test for whether a work qualifies as a 'work made for hire' when created by an independent contractor rather than an employee, making an express assignment clause important whenever work-for-hire status is uncertain.

citation: Stanford University v. Roche Molecular Systems, Inc., 563 U.S. 776 (2011)
note: Confirmed that under U.S. patent law, rights in an invention vest initially in the individual inventor, not their employer, absent an express assignment; a contract that discusses inventions without an assignment clause leaves ownership with the inventor by default.
